coredata.dc:title: Tuning gold nanoparticles growth via DNA and carbon dots for nucleic acid and protein detection
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Na
Liu, Shi Gang
Zhu, Yu Die
Liu, Ting
Lin, Shu Min
Shi, Yan
Luo, Hong Qun
Li, Nian Bing
coredata.dc:description: DNA and carbon dots were utilized to co-mediate the growth of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The properties of grown AuNPs were discussed and applied to colorimetric biomolecule determination. When target biomolecules were absent, single-stranded oligonucleotides (ssDNA) were adsorbed on the surface of AuNP seeds. Branched AuNP solution of blue color was obtained after carbon dots reduction. In the presence of target DNA, it hybridized with the ssDNA and double-stranded oligonucleotides (dsDNA) were formed. As a result, a spherical AuNP solution of red color was achieved after growth as dsDNA would not be adsorbed by AuNP seeds. A similar situation was observed with thrombin. The complex of thrombin and its aptamer was formed, giving rise to spherical AuNPs after growth. The target biomolecule determination was realized by virtue of color change, consistent with the peak shift in UV–vis absorption spectrum. The detection limits were determined to be 5nM for target DNA and 1.8nM for thrombin, respectively. The biomolecule sensors showed the requisite selectivity against possible interferents, and the assay for target DNA exhibited the potential for distinguishing single-base mismatch. These results served as the basis for constructing future biomolecule sensors using DNA and carbon dots co-mediated growth of AuNPs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Gold nanoparticles
Colorimetric detection
Nucleic acid and protein

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: AuNPs synthesis mediated by DNA and C-dots
Colorimetric detection of target DNA and thrombin
Characterization of AuNPs mediated by DNA and C-dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DNA and carbon dots co-mediated growth of gold nanoparticles and application for nucleic acid and protein detection.

(A) UV–vis spectra of AuNP solutions synthesized under different conditions. Inset: The photographs of C-dots reduced AuNPs with (ii) and without 160nM P-DNA (i). TEM and HRTEM (the inset) images of grown AuNPs with (B) and without (C) 160nM P-DNA.
(A) UV–vis spectra of AuNP solutions synthesized under different conditions. (B) The photographs of C-dots reduced AuNPs with different DNA sequences (100nM) or without DNA.

(A) UV–vis spectra of grown AuNPs in the absence of P-DNA (curve i), in the presence of 160nM P-DNA (curve ii), and in the presence of hybridized solution of 160nM P-DNA and 160nM T-DNA (curve iii). (B) UV–vis spectra and photographs (inset) of the system corresponding to different concentrations of T-DNA (from a to h: 0, 16, 32, 64, 80, 96, 128, and 160nM), and the photograph (i) of grown AuNPs without DNA. (C) The responses of the colorimetric sensor toward target DNA over the range from 0 to 160nM. (D) Selectivity investigation of the developed sensor for target DNA against M1, M2, M3, and nDNA. The concentrations of target DNA and other mismatched DNA sequences were all 80nM. TEM images of grown AuNPs in the absence of T-DNA (E) and in the presence of T-DNA (F). Herein, the peak shift is the difference of absorption peaks between grown AuNPs with the target and that without a target.

(A) UV–vis spectra of grown AuNPs without TBA (i), with 160nM TBA (ii), and with the complex of 160nM TBA and 40nM TB. (B) UV–vis spectra and photographs (inset) of the sensor corresponding to different concentrations of TB (from a to h: 0, 4, 8, 10, 16, 20, 30, and 40nM), and the photograph (i) of AuNPs without DNA. (C) The responses of the colorimetric system toward TB over the range from 0 to 80nM. (D) The peak shifts of different concentrations of TB (5, 10, and 20nM) in diluted human serum samples (b) and buffer solution (a). (E) Selectivity investigation of the proposed method for TB. Concentrations: TB (10nM), lysozyme (10nM), IgG (50mg/L, ∼0.33μM), and HSA (50mg/L, ∼0.75μM).

head.ce:title: Tuning gold nanoparticles growth via DNA and carbon dots for nucleic acid and protein detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: DNA and carbon dots were utilized to co-mediate the growth of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The properties of grown AuNPs were discussed and applied to colorimetric biomolecule determination. When target biomolecules were absent, single-stranded oligonucleotides (ssDNA) were adsorbed on the surface of AuNP seeds. Branched AuNP solution of blue color was obtained after carbon dots reduction. In the presence of target DNA, it hybridized with the ssDNA and double-stranded oligonucleotides (dsDNA) were formed. As a result, a spherical AuNP solution of red color was achieved after growth as dsDNA would not be adsorbed by AuNP seeds. A similar situation was observed with thrombin. The complex of thrombin and its aptamer was formed, giving rise to spherical AuNPs after growth. The target biomolecule determination was realized by virtue of color change, consistent with the peak shift in UV–vis absorption spectrum. The detection limits were determined to be 5nM for target DNA and 1.8nM for thrombin, respectively. The biomolecule sensors showed the requisite selectivity against possible interferents, and the assay for target DNA exhibited the potential for distinguishing single-base mismatch. These results served as the basis for constructing future biomolecule sensors using DNA and carbon dots co-mediated growth of AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:para: Methods for sensitive analysis of nucleic acids and proteins play important roles in clinical diagnostics, drug discovery, and food safety [1–3]. The development of colorimetric biomolecule sensors has been the focus of numerous research efforts as both semiquantitative and qualitative assessment can be realized in real time without specific instrumentation or equipped laboratory [4–6]. In this type of sensors, the target-driving signals can be judged by the notable color change during the reaction process. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been widely applied to this analytical method because of their tuned optical properties that can be controlled by the surface plasmon resonance [7–9]. The colors of AuNP solutions can range from ruby red to deep blue through controlling the particle size and shape, making the nanoparticles attractive in the development of colorimetric biosensors.

Carbon nanodots (C-dots) recently emerged as a class of nanocarbon with sizes below 10nm and were comprised of discrete, quasispherical nanoparticles [10–12]. Displaying tunable photoluminescence, biocompatibility, and photostability, C-dots become highly attractive as nascent quantum dots, especially for applications in sensing, imaging cells, drug delivery, and photocatalysts [13–15]. C-dots have been reported to possess various functional groups on the surface, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxylic acid, amino, epoxy, and ether [11]. These functional groups on their surface give rise to high hydrophilicity and possibility for functionalization and can be tailored by changing the source and synthesis procedure. C-dots can act as both the reducing and stabilizing reagent based on the presence of rich hydroxyl moieties on their surface [16]. In the study by Wang et al., elemental silver could be obtained by irradiating the mixed solution of C-dots and AgNO3 [17]. Additionally, C-dots have been employed as the reducing and stabilizing reagent for the synthesis of AuNPs [18–20]. Thus, C-dots provide a new platform for the synthesis of metal materials as other hydroxyl-rich ligands, such as ascorbates, citrates, etc.

Herein, we explored the effect of DNA and carbon dots on the growth of AuNPs and the application of colorimetric biomolecule detection. Consistent with previous reports, single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) could be spontaneously adsorbed on the surface of AuNP seeds [21–23]. A blue colored solution of branched AuNPs was achieved after reduction by C-dots. Upon addition of target DNA or thrombin, double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) or the complex of thrombin and its aptamer was respectively formed, both of which would not be adsorbed by AuNP seeds. Hence, a red colored solution of spherical AuNPs was achieved after growth. Thus, we developed a colorimetric biosensor based on the color change, which was expected to provide the foundation for fabricating future DNA biosensors.

DNA oligonucleotides purified by HPLC were provided by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The oligonucleotide sequences utilized in this work consist of probe DNA (P-DNA), target DNA (T-DNA), 1 nt-mismatched DNA (M1), 2 nt-mismatched DNA (M2), 3 nt-mismatched DNA (M3), noncomplementary DNA (nDNA), and the aptamer of thrombin (TBA). All the oligonucleotides were resuspended in Tris-HCl buffer (10mM, pH 8.0), and their sequences are summarized in Table S1. Human thrombin, lysozyme, immunoglobin G (IgG), and human serum albumin (HSA) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) hydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O) was acquired from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents of analytical reagent grade were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagents Factory (Chengdu, China). In the whole experiment, ultrapure water with a specific resistance of 18.2MΩ cm was employed.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using the UV–vis 2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence measurements were performed using a Hitachi F-4500 spectrofluorophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured in KBr pellet with a Bruker IFS 113v spectrometer (Germany). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were taken on a Philips (FEI) Tecnai G2 F20 operated at a voltage of 200kV.

Hydrothermal synthesis of C-dots was prepared based on a reported work with some modifications [16]. Briefly, sodium hydroxide solution (1M) and ethylene glycol were added in 15:1 ratio (v/v) in a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave, followed by heating the mixture at 180°C for 2h. Thereafter, C-dots solution with pale yellow color was obtained. To remove excess reagents, the C-dots solution was dialyzed against water for 48h through a dialysis film (Mw 1000). The resulting solution was lyophilized to obtain the nanoparticles, which were stored at 4°C in a refrigerator before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AuNPs synthesis mediated by DNA and C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: First, AuNP seeds were prepared according to modifications of a previous report [24]. Briefly, 100mL of 0.01% HAuCl4 was added to a 250mL round-bottom flask and brought to a boil under vigorous stirring. Then 3.6mL of 1% sodium citrate was added to the HAuCl4 solution. The flask was heated for an additional 10min, and the heat source was then removed. After stirring for another 15min, red-violet AuNPs would be obtained. Thereafter, to obtain DNA functionalized AuNPs, 100μL of ssDNA was added to AuNP seeds (5μL) and water (335μL), and the mixed solution was incubated for 10min at room temperature. The growth of DNA functionalized AuNP seed was performed at 37°C as follows: 30μL of C-dots (2.4mgmL−1) and 10μL of HAuCl4 (2mM) were sequentially added to the solution. Another two aliquots of HAuCl4 (10μL, 2mM) were added with an interval of 5min. Photographs were taken after the last addition of HAuCl4 for 10min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of target DNA and thrombin
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 50μL of ssDNA (1.6μM, probe DNA (P-DNA) for target DNA (T-DNA) detection, aptamer of thrombin (TBA) for thrombin (TB) detection) was mixed with 50μL of the target molecules with different concentrations, followed by incubation at 37°C for 30min. Thereafter, the above solution was added to AuNP seeds (5μL) and water (335μL). After 10min of incubation, the growth of DNA functionalized AuNP seed was performed as mentioned above. After the last addition of HAuCl4 for 10min, photographs were taken.

C-dots were synthesized as reported by Deng et al. with some modifications [25]. As shown in Fig. S1A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the C-dots displays a strong peak absorption at 261nm and a shoulder at about 320nm, which is attributed to the transition of CC and CO bonds, respectively [26–28]. C-dots show the maximum emission wavelength of 450nm when they are excited at the maximum excitation wavelength of 360nm (Fig. S1A). Additionally, the resulting C-dots exhibit strong excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence emission, which is a general feature of C-dots (Fig. S1B). The TEM measurement reveals that C-dots are monodispersed with size around 3nm (Fig. S1C). The FTIR spectrum was measured to confirm the functional groups on C-dots (Fig. S1D). The peaks centered at 3414 and 1415cm−1 signify the presence of OH bonds [29,30]. The rich hydroxyl groups of C-dots are probably originated from ethylene glycol. The characteristic peaks at 1595 and 1082cm−1 could be assigned to the CO and CO bonds, respectively [31]. These functional groups provide C-dots the ability to function as a reducing and stabilizing reagent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of AuNPs mediated by DNA and C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the reducing and stabilizing ability of C-dots, we discussed the optical properties of AuNPs obtained by adding C-dots to HAuCl4 solution. As seen in Fig. S2A, an absorption peak at 528nm appears in the absorption spectrum of AuNPs, which is in coincidence with the reported plasmon resonance absorption peak of AuNPs [32]. Furthermore, the corresponding fluorescence intensity of C-dots reduces significantly because of AuNPs formation (Fig. S2B). Next, AuNPs mediated by probe DNA (P-DNA) were investigated with C-dots as the reducing and stabilizing reagent. The morphology of AuNP seeds was characterized by the TEM, which shows the spherical nanoparticles with average diameter of 12nm (Fig. S3A). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of AuNP seeds exhibits a maximum absorption at 518nm (Fig. S3B). After reduced by C-dots, the absorbance of grown AuNPs is significantly increased (Fig. 1 A). The nanoparticle solution mediated by DNA is blue, whereas the solution of nanoparticle without DNA is red. The corresponding red shift is observed in UV–vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 1A. In addition, the morphology of resulting nanoparticles was characterized by TEM. Those nanoparticles synthesized with DNA are branched (Fig. 1B), while nanoparticles synthesized without DNA are spherical (Fig. 1C). These results prove that DNA and carbon dots can co-mediate the growth of AuNPs. Similar to the report by Wang et al., DNA is able to control the shape of metal nanoparticles [33].

Single stranded DNA has been reported to bind to citrated coated AuNPs in a sequence-dependent manner [34]. The optical properties of AuNPs mediated by different DNA sequences were investigated (Fig. 2 ). First, we chose three types of 30-mer DNAs consisting of poly A, poly C, and poly T (designated as A30, C30, and T30). The solution of AuNPs synthesized with A30 is blue and shows red-shifted absorption peak compared with that of C30 and T30 (Fig. 2A). It has been found that deoxyadenosine was stably adsorbed on the AuNPs through both the N7 atom and the N6 exocyclic amino [35]. This highly stable adsorption has also been supported by the fact that poly-A DNA strongly binds to AuNPs regardless of the presence of complementary poly-T DNA [36,37]. Thus, it can be concluded that much more A30 are adsorbed onto the AuNP seed surface compared to C30 or T30. Thereafter, the photographs exhibited nanoparticle solutions synthesized with different length DNA molecules (Fig. 2B). We chose three types of 1-mer, 15-mer, and 30-mer DNAs for this assay, which are constituted by poly A, poly C, and poly T (A1, C1, T1, A15, C15, T15, A30, C30, and T30). With increasing length of DNA sequence, more DNA bases are adsorbed onto the surface of AuNP seed, and thus the solutions tend to change from red to purple or blue after growth. Besides, the amount and the way of HAuCl4 addition were discussed (Fig. S4). When the same amount of HAuCl4 is added by dividing into three equal parts, the peak shift reaches the maximum as shown in Fig. S4A. With the same amount of each addition of HAuCl4, we explored peak shift of grown AuNPs as the added times of HAuCl4 increased (Fig. S4B). The maximum peak shift is achieved with the addition of HAuCl4 for three times. In general, the DNA adsorbed on the seeds can act as a template during the growth of nanoparticles. The formation of the branched AuNPs can be attributed to either selective deposition of the reduced gold on nanoparticles bound by DNA or uneven growth of the nanoparticles because of DNA functionalized surface [33,38].

As illustrated in Scheme 1 , DNA and carbon dots were employed to co-mediate the growth of AuNPs, and a colorimetric biosensor was proposed for sensing nucleic acid and protein. AuNP seeds in solution are stabilized by the citrate ions and thus are negatively charged. As flexible ssDNA is partially uncoiled, its exposed bases can attract AuNP seeds despite the negatively charged backbone [23]. Without target biomolecules, AuNP seeds with surface bound DNA acted as the template and grew into branched AuNPs after the reduction by C-dots. The branched AuNP solution was blue with red-shifted absorption peak. However, dsDNA was formed when target DNA was present. And dsDNA would not be adsorbed because of the repulsion between AuNP seeds and the phosphate backbone of dsDNA. After growth, spherical AuNP solution with red color was achieved. The situation with thrombin was found similar to that of target DNA. The aptamer of thrombin can form a G-quadruplex in the presence of thrombin. Upon binding to thrombin, the aptamer folds to a G-quadruplex (rigid structure). The rigid structure prevents the exposure of the DNA bases to the AuNPs, and the high density of negative charges increases the repulsion between the DNA and the AuNPs [39]. Thus, the thrombin bound G-quadruplex structure cannot bind to AuNPs, resulting in spherical AuNPs after growth. Based on the color change and the peak shift in UV–vis absorption spectrum, colorimetric detection of nucleic acids and proteins was realized in this work.

In this assay, AuNPs mediated by DNA were first utilized to the analysis of nucleic acid. Herein, target DNA sequence (T-DNA) was screened from promoter gene of genetically modified organisms [40,41]. As exhibited in Fig. 3 A, the absorption peak of AuNPs mediated by ssDNA (P-DNA, curve ii) is significantly red-shifted compared to that in the absence of DNA (curve i). Branched AuNPs are formed mediated by ssDNA, consistent with the TEM image in Fig. 3E. In addition, dsDNA can be formed in the presence of target DNA, which would not be adsorbed on the AuNP seeds and results in a blue shift in the absorption peak (curve iii). Fig. 3F shows the approximately spherical AuNPs when target DNA is present. The amount of P-DNA on AuNP seeds was optimized to obtain the large difference of the absorption peak (Fig. S5A and S5B). The increase of peak shift is observed with increasing P-DNA concentration. When the P-DNA concentration was increased to 160nM, high peak shift was obtained. Thus, 160nM P-DNA is chosen for the nucleic acid assay.

Fig. 3B presents that the target concentration controls the absorption peak of AuNPs. The absorption peak is gradually blue shifted with increasing addition of target DNA. The peak shift is increased with increasing concentration of target DNA until the saturation of target occurs at a concentration of 160nM (Fig. 3C). There is a linear response to target DNA from 0 to 128nM. The detection limit is calculated to be 5nM based on 3σ/slope (σ is the standard deviation), which is comparable with those of some reported DNA biosensors [42–44]. To investigate the specificity of the experiment, we applied this sensor to different DNA sequences that are completely complementary target DNA, 1 nt-mismatched DNA (M1), 2 nt-mismatched DNA (M2), 3 nt-mismatched DNA (M3), and noncomplementary DNA (nDNA). It is found that target DNA produces much higher peak shift than other mismatched DNA sequences (Fig. 3D). To evaluate whether this method can be applied to complex matrixes, 20-fold diluted human serum samples were spiked with target DNA. The recoveries varied from 98.2 to 106.9% were achieved, underlying the practicality of the developed sensor (Table S2).

To confirm the general applicability of this strategy, we utilized AuNPs prepared with DNA for protein detection. Herein, TB was chosen as a model molecule. When TBA is adsorbed on the surface of AuNP seeds, a blue AuNP solution with red-shifted absorption peak will be obtained after reduction (Fig. 4 A, curve ii). TBA is known as a G-rich sequence, which can be recognized and bound by TB. When TB is added to TBA solution, the quadruplex-thrombin complexes are formed and would not be adsorbed by AuNP seeds. A red AuNP solution is achieved with blue-shifted absorption peak after reduction (curve iii). The TBA concentration was optimized for large difference of the absorption peak (Fig. S6A and S6B). In this assay, TBA of 160nM was chosen for the assay because of the high peak shift it induced. The sensitivity of this study was investigated based on the peak shift of AuNPs (Fig. 4B). As the TB concentration increases, the solution of AuNPs undergoes a color change from blue to red combining with blue-shifted absorption wavelength. The calibration curve for the determination of TB is shown in the inset of Fig. 4C. It depicts good linearity in the working range of TB concentration from 0 to 20nM. The limit of detection is calculated to be 1.8nM (based on 3σ/slope). Additionally, the colorimetric sensor was applied to 20-fold diluted human serum samples to evaluate its practicality. As seen in Fig. 4D, TB induced peak shifts in human serum and the blank solution are similar. The result in Table S3 shows recoveries from 95.6 to 106.05%. This sensing method is also specific for TB detection. Potential interferents such as lysozyme, IgG, and HSA do not produce detectable peak shift compared to the blank solution without TB (Fig. 4E).

In this work, DNA and C-dots were employed to tune the morphology of AuNPs. Colorimetric sensors for target DNA and thrombin were developed with hydroxyl-rich C-dots as the reducing and stabilizing reagent. We reasoned that target biomolecules controlled DNA that interacted with AuNP seeds, resulting in gradual variation from branched to spherical nanoparticles. The basic properties of grown AuNPs were also explored. The AuNP solution showed colors ranged from blue to red with increasing target concentrations, which provided a new platform for colorimetric sensing. Additionally, blue shifted absorption peak in UV–vis absorption spectrum could be utilized to detect biomolecules quantitatively. Different amounts of the target molecules were successfully applied to diluted human serum samples. The requisite sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed method make it an intriguing option for further development as a detection method.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131), the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (No. CSTC-2015jcyjB50001), and the Innovation Foundation of Chongqing City for Postgraduate (CYB16053).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.05.071.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cascaded multiple amplification strategy for ultrasensitive detection of HIV/HCV virus DNA
Electrochemical detection of nucleic acids proteins, small molecules and cells using a DNA-nanostructure-based universal biosensing platform
Electrochemical methods for the analysis of clinically relevant biomolecules
Aptamer-based PDMS-gold nanoparticle composite as a platform for visual detection of biomolecules with silver enhancement

Target-triggered quadratic amplification for label-free and sensitive visual detection of cytokines based on hairpin aptamer DNAzyme probes
Bio-inspired colorimetric detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+ heavy metal ions using Au nanoparticles
Gold nanorods as colorful chromogenic substrates for semiquantitative detection of nucleic acids proteins, and small molecules with the naked eye
Colorimetric detection of small molecules in complex matrixes via target-mediated growth of aptamer-functionalized gold nanoparticles
Photoluminescent carbon nanodots: synthesis, physicochemical properties and analytical applications
Green synthesis of carbon dots from Ocimum sanctum for effective fluorescent sensing of Pb2+ ions and live cell imaging
Fluorescent nanoprobes for sensing and imaging of metal ions: recent advances and future perspectives
Chemically doped fluorescent carbon and graphene quantum dots for bioimaging sensor, catalytic and photoelectronic applications
Hydroxyl-rich C-dots synthesized by a one-pot method and their application in the preparation of noble metal nanoparticles
Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots
Reduced state carbon dots as both reductant and stabilizer for the synthesis of gold nanoparticles
Adsorption dominant catalytic activity of a carbon dots stabilized gold nanoparticles system
Carbon nanodots as ligand exchange probes in Au@C-dot nanobeacons for fluorescent turn-on detection of biothiols
Colorimetric determination of DNA methylation based on the strength of the hydrophobic interactions between DNA and gold nanoparticles
Label-free detection of sequence-specific DNA based on fluorescent silver nanoclusters-assisted surface plasmon-enhanced energy transfer
Colorimetric detection of DNA sequences based on electrostatic interactions with unmodified gold nanoparticles
Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U. S. A.
Preparation and characterization of au colloid monolayers
Electrochemical synthesis of carbon nanodots directly from alcohols
Toward quantitatively fluorescent carbon-based quantum dots
One-step microwave-assisted polyol synthesis of green luminescent carbon dots as optical nanoprobes
Bandgap-like strong fluorescence in functionalized carbon nanoparticles
Novel fluorescent matrix embedded carbon quantum dots for the production of stable gold and silver hydrosols
Carbon nanodots as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection
Graphene oxide: intrinsic peroxidase catalytic activity and its application to glucose detection
New synthesis strategy for DNA functional gold nanoparticles
DNA-mediated control of metal nanoparticle shape: one-pot synthesis and cellular uptake of highly stable and functional gold nanoflowers
Sequence-dependent stability of DNA-modified gold nanoparticles
Base-dependent competitive adsorption of single-stranded DNA on gold
The coordination chemistry of DNA nucleosides on gold nanoparticles as a probe by SERS
Adsorption of DNA onto gold nanoparticles and graphene oxide: surface science and applications
DNA-encoded tuning of geometric and plasmonic properties of nanoparticles growing from gold nanorod seeds
Simple and sensitive aptamer-based colorimetric sensing of protein using unmodified gold nanoparticle probes
A novel fluorescent biosensor for detection of target DNA fragment from the transgene cauliflower mosaic virus 35s promoter
G-quadruplex DNAzyme as the turn on switch for fluorimetric detection of genetically modified organisms
Carboxylic carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for DNA detection
Polydopamine nanospheres: a biopolymer-based fluorescent sensing platform for DNA detection
A facile fluorescence method for versatile biomolecular detection based on pristine α-Fe2O3 nanoparticle-induced fluorescence quenching

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Na Li is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Shi Gang Liu is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. His major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Yu Die Zhu is a BS candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Ting Liu is an MS candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Shu Min Lin is an MS candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Yan Shi is a senior engineer in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.

Hong Qun Luo is a professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. She received her MS degree in environmental chemistry from Sichuan University in 1991 and PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest China Normal University in 2002. During 2006–2007, she was a visiting scholar in Tohoku University, Japan. Her research is focused on molecular spectroscopy and electrochemical sensor.

Nian Bing Li is a professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. He received his MS degree in physical chemistry in 1997 and PhD degree in material science in 2000 from Chongqing University. During 2000–2002, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Fuzhou University, China. Since 2006–2007, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), Korea. His research interests are the developments of electrochemical devices such as chemical sensors and biosensors.
